title: Recovery-Loop Design

content.statement: Lost users can be reactivated if friction is low and relevance is high.
content.question: What's your business's churned-user recovery play? Do you have one at all?
content.equation: RLD = Reactivation × Message Relevance × Ease of Return
sop: Pull a list of everyone who churned or went inactive in the last 90 days.
Segment them by likely reason (price, timing, bad experience, no need) instead of lumping them together.
Rate your current Reactivation rate, Message Relevance, and Ease of Return each 1-10 for your win-back flow (see calculation, page 6).
Write one specific, human win-back message per segment, not a generic 'we miss you' blast.
Remove every extra step between clicking the win-back link and getting value again.
content.explainer: Most founders obsess over acquisition and ignore reactivation. Reactivation often costs 1/10th as much.
questions: What's your business's churned-user recovery play? Do you have one at all?
When was the last time you personally messaged a customer who ghosted you, instead of writing them off?
What's the one-click path back for someone who churned three months ago, does it even exist?

definitions.def: The rate at which customers stop paying or stop using a product.

content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10: Reactivation (how many churned users you're winning back) 6, Message Relevance (how tailored your outreach is) 8, Ease of Return (how few steps to get value again) 9. Score = 6 times 8 times 9 = 432. Higher means your win-back loop is actually converting; lower means churned users are staying gone.
content.closing_statement: The exit door should double as a front door.

caption:
Principle Nº 88: Recovery-Loop Design

Lost users can be reactivated if friction is low and relevance is high. Most founders obsess over acquisition and ignore reactivation.

Ask yourself: What's your business's churned-user recovery play? Do you have one at all?
